title: What Your Gross Margin Percentage Reveals That Your Revenue Figure Cannot
linkedin:
Revenue grows. Profit doesn't follow. That quiet disconnect is one of the most consistent patterns in Australian owner-led businesses.

The figure that explains it is gross margin percentage. It measures the share of each revenue dollar remaining after the direct costs of delivering your product or service are removed, before overhead is touched. Revenue tells you how much flowed through. Gross margin percentage tells you whether each dollar became more or less profitable as it did.

For businesses in the $1 million to $10 million revenue range, a single percentage point of gross margin movement translates to $10,000 to $100,000 in annual profit. A business running four points below where it sat two years ago has surrendered $200,000 a year at $5 million in revenue. The revenue line has not shown that. Neither has the P&L total.

Three patterns explain most of the drift. Input costs rise and the pricing response is delayed. Revenue growth concentrates in lower-margin lines rather than in the highest-margin categories. Scope in service businesses expands without a repricing conversation.

None of those patterns show up in the revenue figure alone.

With twelve months of complete data available at EOFY, now is the natural moment to plot the gross margin trend across three years. That comparison reveals whether the business is becoming more efficient per dollar of activity, or whether quiet drift has been compounding behind revenue that looked healthy.

The percentage is the signal. The revenue figure is not.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

#ProfitabilityAdvice #AustralianBusiness #SMEFinance
facebook:
Revenue growth is easy to celebrate. A softer profit result at year end is harder to explain.

In most cases, the gap traces back to gross margin percentage, the share of revenue remaining after direct costs are covered. When that percentage drifts, even gradually, the impact compounds. Four points of drift at $5 million in revenue is $200,000 less profit per year.

Has your gross margin held up this financial year, or drifted further than you expected?

Full post on what to look for and when to act: https://profit-pulse.com.au/what-your-gross-margin-percentage-reveals-that-your-revenue-figure-cannot

google:
At this time of year, most business owners across Queensland, NSW, and Victoria are reviewing their financials for the year just closed.

Revenue totals and net profit get the most attention. Gross margin percentage often does not, and that gap matters.

Gross margin percentage is the figure that tells you whether your revenue growth is actually making the business more profitable, or whether the gains are being absorbed by cost increases and pricing decisions that have not kept pace. For businesses in the $1 million to $10 million revenue range, a single percentage point change in gross margin translates to $10,000 to $100,000 in annual profit depending on scale.

The most common sources of gross margin drift in owner-led businesses are input cost inflation that was absorbed rather than passed through, revenue growth concentrated in lower-margin lines, and scope creep in service businesses where the fee stayed flat but the deliverables did not.

With twelve months of complete data now available, the post-EOFY window is the right moment to calculate your three-year gross margin trend and understand what it reveals about the business's direction.

ProfitPulse has published a practical guide to this for East Coast SME owners: https://profit-pulse.com.au/what-your-gross-margin-percentage-reveals-that-your-revenue-figure-cannot
